comment_text.sn11.10:1.2: This opening is reminiscent of Ud 1.10, which specifies the location as Suppāraka
comment_text.sn11.10:1.5: The sages may have been virtuous, but they were not above a little realpolitik.
comment_text.sn11.10:1.7: A barbarian (dāsa) who “fancied himself godlike” (Rig Veda 7.18.20), Śambara’s 99 mountain forts were destroyed for their bounty (6.47.23) by Indra on behalf of Divodāsa Atithigva (4.26.3). His mention close to Virocana (SN 11.8) fits the Arthaśāstra, where they are both invoked together (14.3.19). Despite his decisive defeat at the hands of Indra, later legend makes him a formidable foe and master of sorcery (see SN 11.23).
comment_text.sn11.10:5.5: Tāta (“my dear”) here is an endearment of pity (“oh hon”).
comment_text.sn11.10:6.2: This inverts the power of his magic to put people to sleep (Arthaśāstra 14.3.19).

root_text.sn11.10:1.2: “Bhūtapubbaṁ, bhikkhave, sambahulā isayo sīlavanto kalyāṇadhammā samuddatīre paṇṇakuṭīsu sammanti.
root_text.sn11.10:1.3: Tena kho pana samayena devāsurasaṅgāmo samupabyūḷho ahosi.
root_text.sn11.10:1.4: Atha kho, bhikkhave, tesaṁ isīnaṁ sīlavantānaṁ kalyāṇadhammānaṁ etadahosi:
root_text.sn11.10:1.7: Yannūna mayaṁ sambaraṁ asurindaṁ upasaṅkamitvā abhayadakkhiṇaṁ yāceyyāmā’ti.
root_text.sn11.10:1.8: Atha kho, bhikkhave, te isayo sīlavanto kalyāṇadhammā—seyyathāpi nāma balavā puriso samiñjitaṁ vā bāhaṁ pasāreyya, pasāritaṁ vā bāhaṁ samiñjeyya; evameva—samuddatīre paṇṇakuṭīsu antarahitā sambarassa asurindassa sammukhe pāturahesuṁ.
root_text.sn11.10:1.9: Atha kho, bhikkhave, te isayo sīlavanto kalyāṇadhammā sambaraṁ asurindaṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsiṁsu:
root_text.sn11.10:6.1: Atha kho, bhikkhave, te isayo sīlavanto kalyāṇadhammā sambaraṁ asurindaṁ abhisapitvā—seyyathāpi nāma balavā puriso samiñjitaṁ vā bāhaṁ pasāreyya, pasāritaṁ vā bāhaṁ samiñjeyya; evameva—sambarassa asurindassa sammukhe antarahitā samuddatīre paṇṇakuṭīsu pāturahesuṁ.
root_text.sn11.10:6.2: Atha kho, bhikkhave, sambaro asurindo tehi isīhi sīlavantehi kalyāṇadhammehi abhisapito rattiyā sudaṁ tikkhattuṁ ubbijjī”ti.

translation_text.sn11.10:1.2: “Once upon a time, mendicants, several seers who were ethical, of good character, settled in leaf huts on the ocean shore.
translation_text.sn11.10:1.3: Now at that time a battle was fought between the gods and the titans.
translation_text.sn11.10:1.5: ‘The gods are principled, the titans are unprincipled.
translation_text.sn11.10:1.6: We may be at risk from the titans.
translation_text.sn11.10:1.7: Why don’t we approach Sambara, lord of titans, and beg him for a pledge of safety?’
translation_text.sn11.10:1.8: Then, as easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, those seers vanished from those leaf huts on the ocean shore and reappeared in Sambara’s presence.
translation_text.sn11.10:1.9: Then those seers addressed Sambara in verse:
translation_text.sn11.10:2.2: to beg for a pledge of safety.
translation_text.sn11.10:2.3: For you can give them what you wish,
translation_text.sn11.10:3.3: Though you beg me for your safety,
translation_text.sn11.10:4.1: ‘Though we beg you for our safety,
translation_text.sn11.10:4.3: This is what we get from you:
translation_text.sn11.10:4.4: may endless peril come to you!
translation_text.sn11.10:5.1: Whatever kind of seed you sow,
translation_text.sn11.10:5.2: that is the fruit you reap.
translation_text.sn11.10:5.3: A doer of good gets good,
translation_text.sn11.10:5.4: a doer of bad gets bad.
translation_text.sn11.10:5.5: You, my dear, have sown your own seed,
translation_text.sn11.10:6.1: Then those seers, having cursed Sambara, as easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, vanished from Sambara’s presence and reappeared in those leaf huts on the ocean shore.
translation_text.sn11.10:6.2: But after being cursed by the seers, Sambara woke in alarm three times that night.”
